mnemonics.title: SELECT – Search and Extract Logically Every Tuple
mnemonics.content: Search and Extract Logically Every Tuple – reminds that SELECT retrieves data rows from one or more tables based on conditions.

mnemonics.title: INSERT – Insert New Row In Table
mnemonics.content: Insert New Row In Table – indicates adding a new record (tuple) into a table.

mnemonics.title: UPDATE – Uniquely Modify Data Elements
mnemonics.content: Uniquely Modify Data Elements – signifies changing existing column values in one or more rows.

mnemonics.title: DELETE – Destroy Entire Row
mnemonics.content: Destroy Entire Row – means removing a row permanently from a table.

mnemonics.title: S‑I‑U‑D Sequence Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: S I U D = 'Start In University Days' – helps recall the order of the four DML commands.

acronyms.usage: The subset of SQL used to manipulate data: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE.
The standard language for communicating with relational databases; includes DML statements.
A mnemonic for the four basic persistent storage operations; maps to INSERT (Create), SELECT (Read), UPDATE (Update), DELETE (Delete).

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a library catalogue: you search (SELECT) for books (rows) that meet a certain criteria (WHERE clause).
Picture placing a new book on a shelf: you add a new row to the table.
Think of editing a book's details on an index card: you change the information in a specific row.
Imagine pulling a book off the shelf and discarding it: you remove that row permanently from the table.

analogies.analogy: Scanning a restaurant menu and choosing a dish – you read (select) the options and pick the one you want.
Adding a new contact to your phone’s address book – you create a new entry with name, phone, email, etc.
Editing an existing contact in your phone: changing a phone number or address.
Deleting a contact from your phone – removing that entry permanently.
